speaker: Speaker
transcript: Hello. This is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English. I’m Neil.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: And I’m Beth. When I was a boy, I wanted to be a fireman when I grew up.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: How about you, Beth? Did you have any childhood dreams? I wanted to be an astronaut and fly to the Moon… When we’re young most of us have big dreams and plans for the future.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: Unfortunately, as we grow up these childhood dreams often get lost in the adult world of jobs, money, families and careers.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: But not for everyone… Daisy, from New Zealand, and, Herman, from Argentina are two people who decided to follow their childhood dreams.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: They wanted the world to become a utopia – a perfect, ideal society where everyone is happy and gets along with each other.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: In this programme, we’ll be hearing how Daisy and Herman made their dreams come true – not by changing the world, but by changing themselves.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: And, as usual, we’ll be learning some new vocabulary too.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: But before that I have a question for you, Beth. Following your dreams can be tough, but not following them can leave you regretting all the things you wanted to do but didn’t.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: In 2012, Australian nurse, Bronnie Ware, wrote her bestselling book, The Top Five Regrets of the Dying, after interviewing terminally ill patients about their life regrets.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: So, what do you think their top regret was? Was it: a) I wish I hadn’t worked so hard?

speaker: Speaker
transcript: b) I wish I had followed my dreams? or c) I wish I’d made more money?

speaker: Speaker
transcript: Well, I’ll guess it’s b) they wish they had followed their dreams.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: OK, Beth. I’ll reveal the correct answer at the end of the programme.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: The first dreamer we’re going to meet lives in Riverside, a peace-loving community in New Zealand where everyone shares everything.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: Riverside members work for the community’s businesses, including a farm, a hotel and a café.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: All the money they earn is collected and shared between everyone equally.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: Daisy, who was born in East Germany, joined Riverside in 2004.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: Here she explains her belief in sharing to BBC World Service programme, The Documentary.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: What I think I always believed in is that the sharing of resources can provide a group of people with quite a great advantage, but it doesn’t matter how many hours you work or what work you do, everyone is getting the same amount.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: And that is something that many people outside of Riverside struggle with, and where we’re often getting this ‘communism’ label attached to us, because it’s so… it seems so outlandish for people.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: Riverside isn’t a communist community. In fact, people with many different political views live there.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: But Daisy says that local people struggle with the idea that everything is shared.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: If you struggle with an idea, you find it difficult to accept or think about it.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: Daisy also says some local people call Riverside outlandish – strange and unusual.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: Our second group of dreamers are a family - the Zapps.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: In 2000, childhood sweethearts, Herman and Candelaria Zapp, bought a vintage car and set off from Argentina to travel around the world with less than 3.500 dollars in their pockets.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: Twenty-two years and three children later they have visited over a hundred countries, meeting with countless people and experiences on the way.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: Here, Herman Zapp explains to BBC World Service’s, The Documentary, how following his dream has changed him for the better.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: I am so happy with the Herman there is now, that I know now – not the one who wanted to conquer the world, but the one who was conquered by the world.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: I learn so much from people, and it’s amazing how the more you meet people, the more you know stories, how much more humble you become because you notice that you are a beautiful, tiny piece of sand, but a very important piece of sand like everyone is, right?

speaker: Speaker
transcript: After many years travelling, meeting new people and hearing their stories, Herman is more humble – not proud or arrogant.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: He no longer wants to conquer the world – to control it by force; rather, he has been conquered by his experiences.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: Herman compares himself to a beautiful but tiny piece of sand and uses the phrase 'a grain of sand' to describe things which are insignificant in themselves, but at the same time are an important part of the whole.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: Daisy and Herman are rare examples of dreamers who followed their dream and found a happy life, lived without regret – which reminds me of your question, Neil.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: Yes, I asked about Bronnie Ware’s book, The Top Five Regrets of the Dying.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: What do you think the number one regret was, Beth? I guessed it was b) not following your dreams.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: Which was the right answer! Not having the courage to follow your dreams was listed as the top life regret.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: At least we have people like Daisy and Herman to remind us dreams can come true!

speaker: Speaker
transcript: OK, let’s recap the vocabulary from this programme, starting with 'utopia' – a perfect world where everyone is happy.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: If you 'struggle with an idea', you find it difficult to accept. The adjective, outlandish, means strange and unusual.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: 'To conquer' something means to control it by force. Someone who is 'humble' is not proud or arrogant.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: And finally, the phrase 'a grain of sand' describes something which is both insignificant yet somehow important.

speaker: Speaker
transcript: Once again, our six minutes are up. Bye for now! Goodbye!
